ignore-generated-files.label: Ignore Generated Files
ignore-generated-files.summary: Write focused .gitignore rules and verify that generated artifacts remain outside the repository history.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.title: Try it yourself: Create a .gitignore Rule
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.prompt:
Right after the sketch "The Role of .gitignore in Git", practice that exact idea with this task. Create a `.gitignore` rule to ignore all files in the `dist/` directory. Verify that the rule works by checking the status of a file `dist/output.js`.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter. Produce the result for create a .gitignore Rule so it reinforces Ignore Generated Files.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.instructions:

main_sh.content:

# Press Enter, then complete the requested Git task.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
git init -q -b main
git config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '.zoeskoul/
main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Git practice file 1\n' > dist/output.js

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.title: Try it yourself: Untrack a Committed File
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.prompt:
Right after the sketch "Why .gitignore Doesn't Untrack Files", practice that exact idea with this task. A file `config/settings.json` was committed by mistake. Add it to `.gitignore` and untrack it without deleting it from the working directory.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter. Produce the result for untrack a Committed File so it reinforces Ignore Generated Files.

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.title: Purpose of .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a `.gitignore` file in a Git repository?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.hint: Think about what files you want to exclude from version control.

help.concept: The `.gitignore` file specifies which files and directories should be ignored by Git.
help.hint_1: It helps keep unnecessary files out of the repository.
help.hint_2: It prevents clutter from generated or temporary files.

options.a: To track changes in all files
options.b: To ignore specific files and directories
options.c: To delete files from the repository
options.d: To rename files in the repository

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.title: Effect of Adding to .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.prompt: What happens when you add a file to `.gitignore` that has already been committed?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.hint: Consider the difference between tracked and untracked files.

help.concept: Adding a file to `.gitignore` does not untrack it if it has already been committed.
help.hint_1: The file remains in the repository history.
help.hint_2: You need to untrack it manually if it's already committed.

options.a: The file is automatically deleted from the repository
options.b: The file is untracked and removed from history
options.c: The file remains tracked and in history
options.d: The file is renamed in the repository

ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.title: Common .gitignore Patterns
ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.prompt: Which of the following patterns are commonly used in a `.gitignore` file? Select all that apply.
ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.hint: Consider patterns that exclude files or directories.

help.concept: Common `.gitignore` patterns include wildcards and directory names to exclude files.
help.hint_1: Patterns often include wildcards like `*` and directory names.
help.hint_2: Look for patterns that exclude specific file types or directories.

ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.title: Order of Operations for Ignoring a Committed File
ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.prompt: Arrange the steps to ignore a file that has already been committed.
ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.hint: Consider the sequence of adding to `.gitignore` and untracking.

help.concept: To ignore a committed file, add it to `.gitignore` and untrack it.

ignore-generated-files-sketch0.title: The Role of .gitignore in Git
ignore-generated-files-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
In Git, the `.gitignore` file is crucial for managing which files and directories should be ignored by version control. This is particularly useful for excluding generated files, such as build artifacts or temporary files, that do not need to be tracked. 

Worked example: Consider a project where you have a `build/` directory that contains compiled files. You can create a `.gitignore` file with the line `build/` to ensure that all files within this directory are ignored by Git. This prevents unnecessary clutter in your repository and keeps your version history clean.

ignore-generated-files-sketch1.title: Using git check-ignore
ignore-generated-files-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
The `git check-ignore` command is a powerful tool to verify if a specific file or directory is being ignored by your `.gitignore` rules. This command helps you understand why certain files are not being tracked.

Worked example: Suppose you have a `.gitignore` file that includes `*.log` to ignore all log files. You can use `git check-ignore -v debug.log` to check if `debug.log` is ignored and see which rule is responsible for it.

ignore-generated-files-sketch2.title: Why .gitignore Doesn't Untrack Files
ignore-generated-files-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A common misconception is that adding a file to `.gitignore` will automatically untrack it if it has already been committed. However, `.gitignore` only affects untracked files. To stop tracking a file that has already been committed, you need to use `git rm --cached`.

Worked example: If `config.json` was committed by mistake and you want to ignore it, first add `config.json` to your `.gitignore`, then run `git rm --cached config.json` to untrack it while keeping it in your working directory.

ignore-generated-files-sketch3.title: Creating Effective .gitignore Rules
ignore-generated-files-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Creating effective `.gitignore` rules involves understanding the patterns that match the files you want to ignore. You can use wildcards and directory patterns to specify which files should be excluded.

Worked example: To ignore all `.tmp` files in any directory, you can add `*.tmp` to your `.gitignore`. For ignoring all files in a specific directory, such as `temp/`, simply add `temp/` to the file. This ensures that any temporary files or directories are not included in your version control.
